item.title: Making Engineers into Makers, will Make them Money!
item.dc:creator: mgonzales
item.content:encoded: Developing the hands-on skills, and enthusiasm of young people into a productive engineering skill set, and career, is at the core of a program being launched by Pima Community College, Job Path, Xerocraft and InnovEngine.  Marcus Engineering is participating as the voice of the industry, who seeks to hire talented, hands-on engineers.  “The skills gap is becoming a serious problem” stated Dr. Patrick Marcus, president of Marcus Engineering.  “The Universities continue to generate students strong in the science of engineering, but not quite as strong at the practical side of being an engineer.”   A MoU was signed between the parties on Friday May 13th, 2016 in recognition of the partnership and plans moving forward. Through this partnership PCC and University students, as well as community members, will be able to build knowledge that reaches beyond the classroom and into real-world industry. They will have opportunities to learn practical skills, participate in STEM innovation challenges, and team competitions based on AACC/NSF standards, and the all critical networking opportunities for jobs, resources, and talent. By Fall 2016 the planning committee consisting of students, local businesses, and educators, will be looking to establish an innovation space capable of  holding workshops, trainings, and one-to-one support. “I think that the event this morning was one big step forward in improving the capability and employability of Pima Engineering students, and I am honored that Xerocraft has the opportunity to participate, and enrich the lives, and education of those students.” Connor Barickman, President of Xerocraft.   Xerocraft is a hackerspace in Downtown Tucson, which provides an opportunity for community members from all walks of life to come together and design, innovate, and learn, they provide workshops, mentors, a space for learning, and is free and open to the public.   JobPath is a  non-profit workforce development organization that serves the Tucson  community by creating positive change in the lives of motivated adults through job training, skills development, and education.  Our focus is sponsoring unemployed and underemployed local adults of Tucson and Pima County in long-term education and job training programs.   Marcus Engineering provides high-end electronics engineering and product development services.  Their core capabilities include sensor systems, low power wireless, power electronics, and embedded systems for medical, industrial and consumer applications.   InnovEngine is an organization dedicated to creating superior engineering resources through the establishment of a community resource (MIT Fab Lab PLUS) for innovation and collaboration centered around practical engineering skills.

item.dc:creator: mmarcus
item.content:encoded:
Hands-on Science Project: Got Sun?
Wright Elementary School 4 th graders eagerly participated in a STEM “lunch and learn”, followed by a
solar hot dog barbeque! (ADD PIC)
Education in Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) can be enhanced when there is a
partnership between educational professionals who are dedicated to STEM education, and members of
the science and technology community who are willing to visit schools and enthusiastically share their
knowledge, experiences and expertise with the students. At the elementary level, students are very
curious, and open to learning about the fascinating world of science and technology, especially now that
most are using advanced technology in their daily lives, in the form of computers, smart phones, and
Dr. Patrick Marcus, of Marcus Engineering, made his annual visit to Wright Elementary School as part of
his commitment to STEM education. His sense of humor and energetic presentation kept the students
laughing as they learned. The students eagerly raised their hands to answer questions about the sun
and solar power. Their answers showed that they had been well prepared by their dedicated teachers
and principal, and were ready to begin the science project. They listened intently to Dr. Marcus as he
explained how to make their own solar oven. Marcus challenged the students to be the first in his five-
year history of visiting the school, to have everyone make their solar oven correctly on the first try, and
they did not disappoint! Following the construction, the students proudly brought their creations to Dr.
Marcus for his approval. Then it was out to the courtyard for the final phase, a solar barbequed hot dog
lunch! The students followed Dr. Marcus’s advice on how to correctly position their ovens to take
advantage of the sun’s rays, then patiently waited while the hot dogs slowly cooked. The afternoon
experience culminated in a well-deserved meal, and hopefully, an increased enthusiasm for STEM
As a lifelong science and engineering enthusiast, Marcus knows the importance of early encouragement
and exposure to STEM education, to keep future scientists, engineers and mathematicians coming. His
approach is to add a bit of fun and laughter to an endeavor that, to him, is a very serious issue. With the
increasing need in the U.S. for employees prepared in the ever-advancing technology fields, companies
are finding it more and more difficult to find qualified people to fill these positions. As the owner of a
high tech engineering company, Marcus hopes to do his part to encourage students to pursue
education, and eventually careers, in engineering and other high tech areas. To keep his own business,
as well as the country, on the path of technological advancement and excellence, the production of
future scientists, mathematicians, technologists and engineers is paramount.

item.title: Hands-on Science Project: Got Sun?
item.dc:creator: mmarcus
item.content:encoded:
(ADD PIC)
Wright Elementary School 4th graders eagerly participated in a STEM “lunch and learn”, followed by a solar hot dog barbeque! (ADD PIC)
Dr. Patrick Marcus, of Marcus Engineering, made his annual visit to Wright Elementary School as part of his commitment to STEM education. His sense of humor and energetic presentation kept the students laughing as they learned. The students eagerly raised their hands to answer questions about the sun and solar power. Their answers showed that they had been well prepared by their dedicated teachers and principal, and were ready to begin the science project. They listened intently to Dr. Marcus as he explained how to make their own solar oven. Marcus challenged the students to be the first in his x? year history of visiting the school, to have everyone make their solar oven correctly on the first try, and they did not disappoint! Following the construction, the students proudly brought their creations to Dr. Marcus for his approval. Then it was out to the courtyard for the final phase, a solar barbequed hot dog lunch! The students followed Dr. Marcus’s advice on how to correctly position their ovens to take advantage of the sun’s rays, then patiently waited while the hot dogs slowly cooked. The afternoon experience culminated in a well-deserved meal, and hopefully, an increased enthusiasm for STEM education! (ADD PIC)
As a lifelong science and engineering enthusiast, Marcus knows the importance of early encouragement and exposure to STEM education, to keep future scientists, engineers and mathematicians coming. His approach is to add a bit of fun and laughter to an endeavor that, to him, is a very serious issue. With the increasing need in the U.S. for employees prepared in the ever-advancing technology fields, companies are finding it more and more difficult to find qualified people to fill these positions. Marcus, as the owner of a high tech engineering company, hopes to do his part to encourage students to pursue education, and eventually careers, in engineering and other high tech areas. To keep his own business, as well as the country, on the path of technological advancement and excellence, the production of future scientists, technologists and engineers is paramount.

item.title: $300k Up for Grabs at Venture Madness 2017
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded: Investment capital, brilliant ideas, and hungry entrepreneurs were the main dish at Venture Madness this weekend.  Invest Southwest hosted the top 16 of 64 promising startups at their annual Venture Madness bracket challenge in Scottsdale, Arizona, to compete in the pitch competition portion of the head-to-head battle. These companies were competing for a share of a $300,000 investment pool, as well as a $20,000 pool of competition prize money for the top 4 places. Invest Southwest partners closely with angel groups such as Desert Angels, Arizona Technology Investors, and Canyon Angels, as well as the Arizona Commerce Authority and Greater Phoenix Economic Council to provide opportunities to connect investors and entrepreneurs during Venture Madness, and through supporting events year-round. They are connected by a common driving goal to grow the Arizona entrepreneurial ecosystem by attracting new companies to the desert, as well as investing in the emerging and high quality businesses already here. Venture Madness aided in this goal by uniting investors from Arizona and neighboring southwestern states alike this weekend to observe the competition and to offer their expertise. The final four startups this year consisted of AniCell Biotech, Clean Router, TellSpec, and Zcast. “As a tech nerd I got a real kick out of many of the companies. I was thrilled to see neat technologies like Cleanrouter blocking undesirable websites and Zcast enabling podcasting for the regular Joe,” said Dr. Patrick Marcus of Marcus Engineering. Clean Router is a startup that recognized the gaps in internet protection for families with children. The growing concern of what inappropriate content children may stumble upon when online was remedied by their router system that protects all devices within a home from a single filtering solution. Their system offers a variety of features including their 7-layer Intellifilter, detailed log of activity, emailed reports, and keyword blockers for parents to rest assured that the content on their devices is always family friendly. Zcast is entering the market as an interactive app that allows users to podcast from anywhere with an internet connection. While typical podcasting requires extensive knowledge of equipment and processes that can often deter the everyday listener from participating, Zcast sought to streamline the process and create a user-friendly interface in which amateur podcasters can connect. The app works inline with Twitter to send notifications to followers when a podcast is about to start and invites them to join in, much like how radio stations can take live callers. Possibilities for businesses, social groups, and individuals alike are on the horizon as you can now start the conversation with the push of a button. Dr. Patrick Marcus further commented on his experience saying, “Having been a participant in the state’s startup ecosystem since before it was cool, it’s thrilling to see startups that have their acts together. Strong technology, crisp market analysis, strong mentor teams, and outstanding pitches are really putting Arizona on the map and drawing investors.” “I spoke to no less than 4 angels who were planning on putting cash into the final 4 companies,” said Dr. Marcus, “it’s really exciting.” Calling for even more excitement on his behalf, the founder of Twitch and the CEO of Reddit, along with other astounding panelists also made guest appearances at the event.

item.title: It's More Than Just an Internship
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded:
Everyone knows that the word “internship” looks great on a resume, but an internship with a company that values your personal goals and skill development as a professional is far more valuable than words on a page.

I am currently a junior at the University of Arizona studying electrical engineering with a passion for biomedical applications. I want to build a career that makes a positive difference people’s lives and I believe that every opportunity to develop my skills as an engineer brings me closer to that goal. I was a sophomore when I was introduced to Dr. Marcus, president of Marcus Engineering LLC, who is involved in the community of actively engaged engineering students at the University of Arizona. We sat down and discussed what his company is doing as well as my capabilities and my personal interests in biomedical applications. I was very excited to be offered the opportunity to engage in an independent study project where I could develop my skills as an engineer outside of t he classroom.

We discussed several project options that would help me further develop my skills and aid my career goals as well as result in a useful and marketable product.  The project we chose was a low-powered accelerometer device used to detect specifically human generated movement. I started the project from scratch, researching the necessary technology and providing input on how I thought the product should work. I gained invaluable experience in hardware design and assembly. When the semester ended, Dr. Marcus offered me a position as an intern for the summer. I had the opportunity to continue my project, working in software development and product testing for the device that I designed. I was able to seek guidance from experienced and accomplished engineers, access tools further develop my skills, substantially expand my knowledge in engineering, and actively contribute to the work done at Marcus Engineering LLC. I certainly consider this kind of exposure an asset to my education and personal development as a future engineer. Students at the University of Arizona are privileged to have access to high caliber companies that, like Marcus Engineering LLC, are devoted to building a highly skilled community of excellence. Making a priority of seeking involvement with these kinds of companies is a priceless experience for students, benefiting their individual future and promoting a cutting-edge academic community at the University of Arizona.   Marcus Engineering provides high-end electronics engineering and product development services covering the entire product development.  Core capabilities include sensor systems, low power wireless, power electronics, and embedded systems for medical, industrial and consumer applications.  Custom test equipment and process automation are also key competencies.  Marcus Engineering is located in Tucson Arizona near the University of Arizona campus.

item.title: What's Next? Dr. Patrick Marcus Jumps Aboard Accelerator Program
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded: Many entrepreneurs who successfully start there own businesses are often asked: what's next? Well, Startup Tucson held that answer for Dr. Patrick Marcus of Marcus Engineering. Startup Tucson is a 501(c)(3) educational nonprofit organization that brings growth to Southern Arizona through their Idea-to-Impact Pipeline for entrepreneurs and innovators. Justin Williams' goal, as Chairman and CEO, is to unite entrepreneurs and technologists alike through events that provide continued education and mentorship. Through close ties with Desert Angels, a Tucson based nonprofit of accredited investors who seek to invest in early stage companies within the region, they are able to fund projects and programs that enable businesses to succeed. Through a more recent promise of continued funding from the U.S. Small Business Administration, Startup Tucson was able to debut their business support program Thryve Next. Thryve Next is a fist-of its kind business accelerator program designed for companies in the second stage of their development. For companies to be accepted into the Thyrve program, there are a number of criterion that must be met: companies must have been in business for at least two years, have a minimum of $150,000 in revenues, be able to demonstrate that they are able to grow into larger operations, and create job growth as well as economic growth in Southern Arizona. This program is classified as an “accelerator” because it provides workshops and continued education to support the rapid changes that occur in the second stage of growth for companies. Mid-level companies in the accelerator program aim to see their company rise to the next level, and do so through the guidance that Thryve Next provides. Dr. Patrick Marcus, President of Marcus Engineering, was a member of the inaugural Thryve Next class and has been able to grow his startup business successfully over the years in part through his continued education and contribution to programs such as this. The program consists of a series of intensive workshops and courses, meant to re-think your businesses strategy and goals, and teach business owners how to frame their operations for future success. The program concludes with a demo day in which the business owners give presentations on their new pathway to success for future growth. Dr. Patrick Marcus demonstrates his company’s keys to success, business model, and plans for future growth in his Thryve Next graduation pitch below.   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ffMpjaT9Ovg   Thryve is a social impact incubator providing entrepreneurs the tools to take their innovations from idea to impact. They provide a structure program of venture concept development based on lean startup principles, coaching with experienced mentors, a community of motivated entrepreneurs, opportunities for expanding your professional network, and the strategy and pitch training necessary to access capital.

item.title: Marcus Engineering Brings Home a Copper Cactus
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded:
Marcus Engineering was recently awarded a Copper Cactus Award in celebration of their accomplishments and continued innovation; a recognition awarded to Southern Arizona’s most notable locally owned businesses and charitable non-profit organizations! The Copper Cactus Awards are presented by Wells Fargo and various other sponsors through the Tucson Metro Chamber each year. Marcus Engineering was awarded a Copper Cactus Award this year in the Nextrio Innovation category. Nominees in this category were judged based on their creative use of innovative processes or products to pioneer change in their industry. Marcus Engineering, being at the forefront of innovation and design technologies, has proudly earned this prestigious recognition. The Tucson Metro Chamber describes itself as a membership-based business advocacy and networking organization that represents over 1,500 businesses, with small businesses making up approximately 60 percent of membership. They advocate for the interests of businesses at all levels by developing a climate in which businesses can create jobs while continuing to operate at a profit. The Chamber’s work is funded entirely through membership investments, which are encouraged by their track record of over 120 years of proven healthy growth. The programs put on by the Chamber are member-run, capitalizing on the expertise and experience of local business owners. Through participating in these committees, as well as various other networking and special events, members continue to see growth in their businesses, as well as the Tucson community. The Tucson Metro Chamber goes further than fostering growth in existing businesses in Tucson by attracting businesses to relocate or do business here in the desert. This incites economic growth by creating jobs and bringing new innovation to our community, while further stimulating our existing entrepreneurial ecosystem. Further on the topic of new talent and growth, they have enacted an Intern to Career project, which fills the gaps in workforce development that have proven to be evident for existing members. This goal is not unique to the Chamber, as Marcus Engineering aims to fill the same gaps in its contributions through its workforce development of young talent. However that is just one piece of Marcus Engineering’s work and dedication to the community. Dr. Patrick Marcus, President of Marcus Engineering, describes his company and how they have come to achieve this success below:

“Marcus Engineering is a Tucson-grown advanced electronics products development company. We are technology driven and leverage cutting-edge electronics and software solutions to implement product designs for our customers. Marcus Engineering designs medical instrumentation, Internet-of-Things, and other highly reliable systems of industrial, military, medical, and mining industries. Electronics, product design, rapid-prototyping, and business marketing insight make Marcus Engineering a one-stop shop for high quality, market-driven, electronics-based product solutions. We are also experts in industrial automation, and test equipment development. Dedicated to the community, Marcus Engineering invests over 700 hours per year educating, invigorating, and challenging Tucson’s high-tech entrepreneurial economy.”

item.title: TLA Commercialization Partners Bring Fresh Talent to the Market
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded:
Tech Launch Arizona aids in bringing the inventions and discoveries stemming from university research to the commercial market through many supports; one of which being its Commercialization Partners through its Commercialization Network. TLA Commercialization Partners act as liaisons and mentors to link the University of Arizona's talent to the tech-business community, and give expert guidance along every step of the way. Our own Dr. Patrick Marcus, President of Marcus Engineering, serves as one of twelve on this board, and fills the role of Executive-in-Residence. Dr. Patrick Marcus collaborates with the talent in this organization on a weekly basis to provide his valuable feedback  and mentoring in order to help link the existing skills and talent to the leadership and initiative needed to take their work to the marketplace. Here, he works with the team to identify commercial potential and inputs his entrepreneurial perspective in  order to help those individuals maximize their works' potential. Pictured below is Dr. Marcus working with other members of the TLA network on some of their initiatives, many of which have achieved success recently. With the help of the TLA Commercialization Partners, Tech Launch Arizona achieved all of this in 2016:
  • 14 new startup companies
  • 250 invention disclosures
  • 278 U.S. patents filed
  • 27 asset development awards ($1.22M)
  • $2,016 royalty income (in thousands)
This, coupled with significant past achievements, has led TLA as partners with the University of Arizona to reach new heights in the tech-business marketplace. However, there is still work to be done. TLA Vice President, David Allen, refers to this as "just a starting point for what TLA targets to become an extensive effort to grow the commercialization ecosystem." He says, "As we bring on more commercialization partners, these top entrepreneurs and executives will participate in technology pathway discussions across the whole of TLA and make great contributions to the success of these ventures." Dr. Patrick Marcus continues to aid in the contributions of TLA, drawing from his own experiences. His company Marcus Engineering contributes greatly to the entrepreneurial ecosystem of Tucson in many avenues similar to this. One of Marcus Engineering's, and Dr. Marcus', most important goals is to bridge the critical skills gap between classroom education and employment for young engineers. It is clear how this initiative enables him to do just that, and we cannot wait to see what the partners accomplish next.

item.title: Makers Toil in Solitude no More
item.dc:creator: lmiller
item.content:encoded: Here in the Tucson Community, there are a number of organizations dedicated to feeding the everlasting appetite of young makers and hackers, one of which is Xerocraft. Xerocraft is a registered 501(c) (3) nonprofit corporation that acts as a collaboration space; providing an extensive resource base of tools, equipment, supplies, and knowledge for the people who like to do it themselves, or have an amazing idea but lack the know-how. They have been steadily growing over the last 5 years in providing a membership-based physical space and resource bank for our local makers and hackers to explore. Simply stated, Xerocraft is the paid gym membership for creators. Community hacker spaces enable individuals to grow their skill sets in an informal setting and find their passions through hands-on creation and invaluable collaboration. “The maker movement, as we know it, is the umbrella term  for independent inventors, designers and tinkerers. A convergence of computer hackers and traditional artisans. The niche is established enough to have its own magazine, Make, as well as hands-on Maker Faires that are catnip for DIYers who used to toil in solitude. Makers tap into an American admiration of self-reliance and combine that with open-source learning, contemporary design and powerful personal technology like 3-D printers. The creations, born in cluttered local workshops and bedroom offices, stir the imaginations of consumers numbed by generic, mass-produced, made-in-China merchandise.” - Adweek Dr. Patrick Marcus, president of Marcus Engineering, recognizes that many traditional students who stick solely to classroom learning fall victim to a substantial skills gap upon joining the workforce. Marcus Engineering both appreciates and encourages the type of learning you find at community hacker spaces, as it lends to continued success for employees. "You can't design if you can't build!" is a comment often made by Dr. Marcus who has an extensive background as not only a doctoral level engineer but also as a hands on engineer and technician. James Wyeth, a past intern of the company, knows first-hand the kind of impact hacker communities can make.  James was a part of the Bit Buckets robotics team at Xerocraft, but also used the space for personal projects outside of that scope.  “Xerocraft provided the tools and expertise to teach me practical skills. As a high school student I did not have any money to get the tools myself, and even if I did it probably would not have been worth it for me to get them at that time in my life," He says. "My school was very academic based, so it did not have any of those resources either. If it was not for Xerocraft, I would have gone to college with very few mechanical skills, and it would have taken a long time for me to acquire them.” Supporting local hacker spaces will further empower makers of all ages and abilities to create, learn, and further stir the imaginations of people everywhere. Our society is on the cusp of a new age of invention; bridging resources to the marketplace and forging a new pathway in which individuals can find success without having to play by the rules. This sort of collaborative production redefines the way in which we view creativity and creation.  It allow us to go back to a time in which creating something with our own two hands was more valuable than picking something off of a shelf. Companies like Marcus Engineering are contributing to the growing environment of makers by recognizing their value and empowering employees to create outside of normal work hours. With this kind of continued support, those engineers will never stop hacking and making, and we will continue to see our world’s biggest problems being solved here in our local communities. Engineers who enjoy engineering as a passion and as a hobby are always the most successful and strongest contributors.  By investing in local maker spaces, we can assure the best talent for our workforce. When makers will no longer toil in solitude, they build a community that they want to remain in.

item.title: The Future Talent at McGuire Summer Academy
item.dc:creator: eford
item.content:encoded: This year, Dr. Patrick Marcus, was invited to be a guest speaker at the McGuire Summer Academy Program at the University of Arizona. The McGuire Summer Academy Program is a program designed to help young people cultivate their entrepreneurial skills. Students from various high schools are invited to live on the campus grounds for one week. Throughout the week, students work in teams and develop business ventures from an idea to a fully-fledged pitch. At the end of the week, students compete in front of a panel and pitch their business venture ideas. Afterwards, the winning team is awarded 300 dollars.  Throughout the week each team would walk through a new aspect of the business venture process with the help of a guest speaker. After each speech, participants from each team were allowed a one-on-one session with these speakers. From there, they would receive the insight and feedback they needed to strengthen their lean business canvas. Although these students had only one week in this program, the skills they learned encompassed a great deal of subjects from business finance, to social networking, to the art of the business pitch. Dr. Marcus participated as a mentor and guest speaker throughout this week. “I’ve participated in many university programs in the past,” says Dr. Marcus, “so I was really excited when I was asked to come back and help out. You never know the kind of talent that can come from your own hometown, and these kids certainly have got talent in spades." According to Dr. Marcus, we can expect to see some truly innovative ideas from theses students. Well done to the participants of this program. We can't wait to see and hear more from you in the future. Check out the video below to see last year's participants in the McGuire Summer Academy. [embed]https://youtu.be/3z9YmKzTdKA[/embed]

item.title: STEM Education: What Needs to Change?
item.dc:creator: eford
item.content:encoded:
Like most people, I was never great at math or science. I was average at best during elementary school and struggled with it in high school. I was discouraged, disheartened even. Every report card ever sent to my house made my stomach churn with anxiety. The situation today hasn’t changed. If you ask your neighborhood kids these days whether or not they like their science or math class, most of them will tell you they don’t. But why? Why do so many kids have this aversion to science and math? It’s because they’ve been conditioned to.   Unfortunately for most kids, the reason certain classes aren’t interesting to them is due to the fact that they aren’t relatable. It is the natural instinct of all human beings to obtain knowledge that is applicable to their individual lives. If they receive information that cannot be applied to their own lives, they won’t invest putting any effort into keeping that information. Ultimately, they will dispense all of that information because it isn’t relevant to their needs. Because of this, there is an immediate disconnect between the student and the material. If the student isn’t invested in the information, why would they bother storing it in their memory? The most problematic issue, of course, is the fact that curriculum in the United States varies quite drastically. Each state has different focuses, each school district has different lesson plan requirements, each school teaches different material. The knowledge gap between schools, even within the same state, is so wide that students are often lost in the transition from one grade or one school to another. The difference between national curriculum and school district curriculum may not seem that different up close, but if you take a closer look the amount of differences between lesson material is extremely apparent.  In the United States, there are no officially set national standards. There are multiple organizations who claim to have set and regulate "the national standard" but it's more or less no different from many state standards. If you were to conduct some research on comparative state standards, you will find that each state develops its own standards based on statewide systems. On top of that, often times when state standards undergo changes in curriculum teachers must change their lesson material to reflect those changes, resulting in information to pass to one class of students and not to another. As a result, the newer generation of students will have learned information that the previous generation never even covered. As a child, I moved around a great deal. In my lifetime I have lived in three different states and attended seven different schools. In fact, there was a period of time where I went to a different school every year for three years. In that time, I realized just how vastly different each school was in lesson material and teaching methods. In the space of two months, I went from an A student to a C student because I simply did not have the base knowledge to understand the material from the previous school I attended. On top of the disparity of school curriculum, there is the issue of utilizing different mediums and methods of teaching to connect to all students. We’re all familiar with the different types of learning and the tests we take to determine how best we learn. Visual, auditory, linguistic, and kinesthetic may be the core types of learning in the world of education but often times teachers only teach lesson material in their style of learning. In other words, a teacher may have learned this material from a linguistic perspective and they only teach the material from a purely linguistic learning style. This isn't particularly helpful to students who are auditory learners or kinesthetic learners. The majority of teachers don’t cater to the different kinds of learning that their students require in order to understand the material. Without incorporating resources, tools, and elements from each learning style students cannot gain the full picture of the lesson material. To use myself as an example, I realized that I am a combination of linguistic and kinesthetic learning. So, when I began to struggle in my math classes in school, I used sign language to help me remember formulas and work out complex equations. Lastly, perhaps the most interesting issue to overcome, is the fact that classrooms aren’t adaptable. Now, obviously there are regulations and restrictions that need to be implemented throughout any school. However, when the classrooms cannot adapt to the lesson material students cannot learn how to mold their learning styles to memorize and apply information. Resources and tools need to be readily available to classrooms just as often as they need to be available to students. It is our responsibility to teach students how to adapt in a school environment so that they can utilize those skills to adapt to other situations in their own lives and in their future careers. So, now that we’ve established the major challenges, what needs to happen in order to overcome these challenges? The major issue to address is the disparity in school curriculum. Until we can decrease the knowledge gap from school to school, it’s going to be difficult for students to transition and perform at their peak. The only way to do that is by creating a system within each state that helps bring school districts closer together in terms of the lesson material.  Now, obviously there is no immediate way to bring all schools throughout the nation closer to a centralized curriculum, but at the very least, we can provide schools within the same city the kind of attention they need to bring the knowledge gap to a close. Secondly, teachers need to address all types of learning styles and structure their teaching methods to reflect those needs. Every student learns differently. It is true that there may be some overlap between students in terms of learning style combinations, but at the end of the day every student has a different mind. It is of the utmost importance to remember this because as teachers, we have the power and capacity to change students’ perceptions of the world. So incorporate more than just occasional videos and power-point presentations, because those will only go so far. Get your students to actively participate in the lesson material.  Create games, create discussion groups among your students, bring in interesting guest speakers to show students what it’s really like to work in that field. Engage them directly by giving them access to resources, materials, and people they didn’t know were available to them. If you show a vested interest in helping them determine how best they learn, they will take the initiative to learn the material. Finally, and perhaps most importantly, if students are going to show even the most remote amount of interest in STEM, knowledge needs to be relatable. Odds are, if you start talking about theoretical physics to a room of fourteen year old kids without any connection between the science and the real world, you’re going to lose them before you’ve even started getting into the meat of the material. If students don’t have some sort of reference point to an aspect of the real world, it’s going to be extremely difficult for them to understand material. A student can memorize any fact you give them, but if they can’t relate it to something within the world, they will never fully understand it. Memorization is one thing, comprehension is another. Students have the potential to become the greatest, innovative talent of tomorrow but they need the right encouragement, the right resources, and the right people to help them. If we can engage them in the classroom and cater to their needs to help them learn and understand their strengths and weaknesses, we can help them realize their potential to pursue STEM careers. Our students have the talent and the drive, what we must do is create an environment where both talent and drive can be nurtured. References: Check out these additional links to learn more about how to get kids interested in STEM careers.
  1. https://www.thoughtco.com/state-versus-national-standards-7766
  2. http://lifehacker.com/how-to-get-your-kids-interested-in-stem-without-forcin-1751361598
  3. https://www.livescience.com/43296-what-is-stem-education.html
  4. http://blog.iat.com/2015/08/13/stem-for-elementary-school-students-how-to-instill-a-lifelong-love-of-science/
  5. http://www.edweek.org/tm/articles/2014/06/17/ctq_jolly_stem.html

item.title: Keeping Up in a Constantly Changing World
item.dc:creator: eford
item.content:encoded:
1. Data information?
  2. Innovation
  3. Startup burst
Have to think about this in some more detail but I think this could be a good one.

item.title: The Obstacles of IoT
item.dc:creator: eford
item.content:encoded:
The Internet of Things, otherwise known as IoT, has received a tremendous amount of attention within the past few years. Simply put, IoT, is the concept of creating an interconnected world of devices and consumer products that send and receive data through one central system (re work this sentence, I still don't like it, perhaps look for a definition that you can quote? although that might discredit us a bit).  So, if one day, you were to purchase a watch that was connected to your smart fridge, the data exchange between those two devices would be processed in the cloud to determine things like, the kind of groceries you purchase or even document temperature changes in your freezer. Sounds incredible, why hasn't this been implemented in all of our devices and homes today? Well, it's not entirely an easy system to implement. As developments in IoT begin to barrel down the timeline, there are several challenges that pose a great threat to IoT and, more specifically, to businesses producing and selling IoT products.
  1. Security - Perhaps the most difficult challenge to overcome in the age of big data, data collection, and data analysis; is the issue of security. The issue resides in the fact that IoT devices are, at the moment, not impervious against hacking. What's even more difficult is that there is no definitive way to detect when an IoT device is being hacked when there are several hundred billion other IoT devices on the planet. There is an incredibly high risk of data hacking in IoT devices, that there is no set-in-stone method of combating it. This is largely attributed to the fact that most companies that make IoT products are often focused so intensely on delivering a product to the marketplace that security of said product tends to take the backseat. Not to mention, because of the wide variety of businesses producing IoT products, there is no way to ensure which devices will have worse, better, or equal security system in place.
  2. Privacy - The concept of IoT also presents a series of challenges for privacy. These issues tend to go beyond the point of information privacy and include integrated systems that consumers utilize in their own homes. Which puts the security of the consumer's home or business or other environmentally integrated system at risk. This makes the issue of data privacy and domestic privacy even more challenging. This presents a series of issues in creating back up security systems for individual houses and businesses as well as an issue of connecting security services of those systems to the IoT cloud.
  3. Connectivity - Connecting a few billion devices around the globe is a tall order, especially given the current communication models in place. Currently, we rely on a model of centralized servers that exchange information between different nodes in a network. This, of course, is a major issue in IoT technology because it is an insufficient model to utilize on a global scale. When a network grows and attempts to support billions of devices, this centralized server becomes a narrow passage for data to pass through, and therefore cannot be analyzed efficiently. Essentially, the data between devices becomes bottle-necked and it causes data streaming issues. Data can get lost or simply take too long to process. (Need to work on this part later).
  4. Compatibility - Device compatibility is not a new concept in the tech industry. However, when it comes to IoT devices, the issue of compatibility is far more challenging. With so many different companies looking to build and produce IoT devices, there is no standard cloud service or even hardware and software to seamlessly connect devices. In addition to the disparity between different business devices, there is the issue of availability to newer versions of these IoT devices. As devices are revamped by companies, there is an issue of the compatibility of newer versions vs. older versions of said technology. So despite the fact that you might own the newest version of an IoT device, the devices used by the people you try to connect with may not be compatible to share or send data to you. Additionally, if your wearable device is meant to sync up with your house to turn on the lights, there may be issues if your newer device is incompatible with the current systems in your house.
  5. Analysis - The purpose of any IoT product is not only to cater to the consumer's needs of automation but also to acquire and analyze data to anticipate future trends. Of course, with so much data to analyze there a number of potential systems that could be utilized to achieve data acquisition and analysis on such a large scale. The majority of IoT leaders are expressing the possibility of using intelligent machine learning interfaces to analyze data to predict future consumer behaviors. The problem with that, at least currently, is the fact that these intelligent systems are fairly unpredictable. If these systems do not receive a large quantity of data they are likely to produce inaccurate analyses.
Conclusion - The possibilities of IoT are staggering and there is a great deal of promise in IoT endeavors, but the fact remains that until there are solutions to combat these challenges, it cannot grow into a universal system in the immediate future. Of course, we all secretly yearn for a world in which IoT. However, there are a number of risks that could pose serious security dangers. Additional Links: Resources
  1. http://iot.ieee.org/newsletter/march-2017/three-major-challenges-facing-iot.html
  2. http://iot.ieee.org/newsletter/january-2017/iot-and-blockchain-convergence-benefits-and-challenges.html
  3. https://www.sitepoint.com/4-major-technical-challenges-facing-iot-developers/
Possible Photos to Use - https://www.pexels.com/photo/nature-laptop-outside-macbook-6508/ - Like this one because it shows the connection of the devices https://www.pexels.com/photo/finger-print-on-enter-key-of-keyboard-163017/- Like this one because it highlights one of the issues of IoT https://www.pexels.com/photo/smartwatch-apple-watch-device-time-9050/ - Like this one because it shows a current IoT device that is current

item.title: Requirements Why? What? How?
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
There is often confusion among our customers regarding how to formulate a contract, a statement of work and an engagement for design services.  The confusion often lies in 3 simple question “Why? What? and How?” Making these three questions clear to your consulting firm can greatly streamline the engagement process, will reduce costs, and alleviate miscommunication and frustration. One secret to lower pricing is being on top of these principles.  A company that comes across as experienced with product development, communication, and requirements planning will always convey to the customer  lower risk and smother development, meaning  a decrease in both cost and frustration.

WHY?

Sometimes customers are reluctant to get into the details of the WHY.  The WHY includes the market, the customer, and the business reasons for developing the product in the first place.  If you are concerned about leaking the secrets of your business model and customer, don’t be.  Your consultants have their own business to run and industry to deal with.  They don’t want to steal your ideas.  Additionally, imagine how many customers a consultant will have if they are not acting in good faith..  Furthermore, NDAs are likely in place at this stage, offering reasonable protection for sensitive information. Frequently, customers don’t convey the WHY of a project.  If you simply want a circuit board made, a widget machined or a software module written, you may think that your consultants do not  need to know the WHY.  I’d advise against that approach.  The value of a consulting firm like Marcus Engineering is decades of expertise in product development, due diligence, and product market fit, in addition to the raw design effort.  The more we understand about WHY you are developing the product, the better we will be able to advise the design process, make the right tradeoffs, and point out needs, features, or challenges that you might not have thought of.  You can never have too much information to build decisions around!

WHAT?

The WHAT conveys to your consulting firm exactly what your product needs to do.  Where most customers trip themselves up is by not prefacing the WHAT with the WHY for the product.  The product context may seem obvious to you, as the customer, who has been working in your industry and on your product for many months or years, but it is new to your consulting firm.  Make sure you give context to the WHAT with the WHY. The WHAT includes basic, but important contexts for the product development, estimating, schedule, and deliverables.  System, Functionality, User experience and Environmental requirements are just some of the basics that need to be considered.  Marcus Engineering has several guides on requirements that we are happy to share with potential customers.  JUST ASK US.

HOW?

This is where customers may sometimes  go overboard.  The HOW is the specific implementation executed for meeting the requirements.  The how is often referred to as the “Specification”.  Examples would be the “Hardware Specification” or “Software Specification”.  Customers often get overzealous with the HOW, telling the consultants what microprocessors to use, what technologies are needed and which protocols they desire. Making suggestions can be productive, and often the customer has a good idea, BUT it is also advisable  to give the consulting firm the opportunity to present multiple options for meeting requirements.  Remember, the consultants are often experienced experts in the technology space.  You will get the most value by letting them propose solutions rather than dictating a specific path. Another pro tip is to leave  the HOW open ended; by doing so, you can learn a LOT about your prospective consultants.  Pick their brains about technology options, complexities, risks, why one approach is better than another.  You will learn a lot about their capabilities as well as picking up details about the technology space you might not have been aware of. Remember: WHY? WHAT? HOW?  Share the WHY, don’t be shy.  WHAT is the most important and formulates the  understanding between you and the consulting firm.  Finally, keep the HOW open ended and use it as your opportunity to learn and vet your consultants!

item.title: Mark's Qi Charging Class and Calculator
item.dc:creator: jrowley
item.content:encoded: Mark Omo will be giving a presentation on Qi Wireless Charging at Microchip Technology's 2019 Worldwide MASTERs conference, along with Santosh Bhandarkar from Microchip. Their class is 23104 PC12: Fundamentals of Wireless Power, and if you'll be at MASTERs this year, you should check it out. To accompany his presentation, Mark developed a spreadsheet that automatically calculates the Q factor and the necessary capacitors for a Qi antenna, given the parameters of the antenna coil. You can download a copy by filling out the form on our Qi Charging Calculator page.

item.title: Mentorship: An engineer and a creative
item.dc:creator: dgunn
item.content:encoded:
Twice a year, Tucson Young Professionals (TYP) partners with Southern Arizona Leadership Council (SALC) to provide mentorship to Tucson’s best, brightest, and most motivated future leaders in our community. The selected mentees are paired with experienced regional leaders and CEOs for a twelve-week program that is a terrific learning experience for both the mentor and mentee.


Dr. Patrick Marcus was recently paired with Malcom Critcher, Associate Creative Director for BRINK. BRINK is a creative agency for activist brands that has content studios in Washington, DC and Tucson, AZ. Over the past several months, Dr. Marcus and Malcom met on a weekly basis and discussed new challenges and opportunities to grow as leaders. “At times, it can feel like our growth is predetermined by the leaders and individuals we know, or the sector in which we work. The TYP mentorship program provided us with an opportunity to break outside of that, and for an Engineer and a Creative to both look at leadership in a new light.” Said Malcolm about the experience.


“The mentoring/mentorship opportunity is like anything else in life, what you put into it is what you get out of it. The learning never stops”. TYP’s curated mentorship program has weekly themes on topic areas to connect, grow, and thrive. The themes focus on varying career, personal, and career goals such as; understanding themselves, understanding their community, Personal SWOT analysis, setting SMART goals, personal narrative, professional development, networking, and much more!


Malcolm is an established Creative Director with many years of experience in a leadership position. Having recently made a shift to a more traditional organization compared to freelance capacity, he was provided with new opportunities to grow as a leader. “Patrick was the perfect pairing for this mentorship experience. A steady handed leader who puts people first, the insights he shared were countless, and invaluable.”


The mentorship relationship is a two-way street, and the mentor gets just as much out of the investment as the mentee. Malcom shared, “I went into this experience expecting to spend most of my time listening, but it always felt like a continuous exchange of information. We learned from each other, shared in one another’s weekly struggles as leaders, and helped each other grow. I left every conversation we had with a grounded, but high-level piece of advice that was applicable across the board as the leader of an organization.”


Dr. Patrick Marcus has been in industry for nearly two decades, and the hard-knock lessons of firsthand experience can sometimes really knock the wind out your sails. “I’ve been fortunate to have some really great mentors and advisors over the course of my career to bounce ideas off of and helped me navigate the hard times.” Some of the top key lessons that Dr. Patrick Marcus has learned over the years are:


Don’t be afraid to fail. Failure is part of the learning process. In engineering, we want to fail fast and often early because that means our learning curve will flatten out much more quickly.


Build your network. It is never too early start building your network. Your network is the net of resources throughout your career that helps create opportunities and supports your through the challenging times.


Help others succeed. In a tech ecosystem, we are all connected. Altruism is a key element in professionalism, and is essential to economic growth. By helping others succeed you are strengthening your brand and your community. We all need help, and we should all be ready to give and receive it.

item.title: Do you have what it takes to be a Marcus Engineering Employee?
item.dc:creator: ecary
item.content:encoded:
Think you would make a valuable contribution? Take this short test to find out...
  • You own multiple Arduinos
  • You have spent more than an hour arguing about spaces vs tabs
  • You constantly prowl Ebay and Auctions for test equipment
  • You know FPGA is not a DOD program
  • Your boss has attended D&D night
  • You have a home electronics bench
  • You argue that PIC16 is way better than ATMega (and always will be)
  • Hab SoSlI’ Quch!
  • Your test equipment costs more than your car
  • You hacked your Rigol scope to provide upgraded bandwidth
  • “\n”.join(“Lol” for _ in range(100))
  • You make animatronic unicorn heads
  • Your boss put a tub of Costco Peanuts on your desk as a “joke”
  • You have eaten an entire tub of Costco Peanuts as a “joke”
  • You spent your bonus on PCBs for a personal project
  • You have not seen the top of your desk in a month
  • You end up destroying things just to see how they work
  • Pickle Rick
  • You have spent more than hour thinking of a funny acronym for a project
  • Your office features a My Little Pony canvas
How many did you check mark? If you checked 0 . . . . No worries! There is definitely another company who will appreciate your skills. If you checked about 5 . . . . You may be intimidated by how passionate we are. Our love for what we do can be pretty intense. If you checked >8 . . . . You belong here!!!! Click this link to send in your resume right now!!! Please, we want you!! If you checked all 20 . . . . You already work here. I know it's you reading this, Kempton.

item.title: Ready for Next Gen Hiring Practices?
item.dc:creator: ecary
item.content:encoded: Pre-Employment Assessments are one of the newest forms of interviewing techniques, used by companies big and small. There are multiple types of these tests but they generally fall under two categories: Personality or Skillset. The Personality Pre-Employment Tests are used heavily in the Sales Industry to provide the interviewer with an unbiased, objective opinion about an applicant. Depending on the job, the test is a combination of the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator, cognitive thinking, and other skills.  Indeed, one of the most commonly used employment-related search engines, has recently implemented their own version, "Indeed Assessments." As we work to hire the best and brightest, as well as finding people who are the right “fit” we created a "Strongman" game for students to attempt at the 2018 University of Arizona Career Fair. Designed to attract students to our table with bright, colorful lights, it also functioned as a Skillset-based Interview Test. We provided candidates with an op-amp, resistors, and a power source Anyone brave enough to ask was told they need to hit a specific target voltage. When the correct voltage is produced, the game lights up and the applicant is deemed “Intellectually” strong while also possessing the ability to ask questions, demonstrate technical confidence, assimilate information, think under pressure, and show persistence. We quickly saw  how individuals went about attacking the problem: Did they ask specific questions in an attempt to solve the problem? Or did they simply ask how to do it? When they couldn’t figure it out, how quickly did they give up? Was there an attempt to solve the problem using their resources, like the internet? How did they react when frustrated? Successful? How willing and able were they to learn and grow? It is very important to our company that employees have a good culture fit, as well as the correct skillset. By simply observing students attempt to solve the problem, we could easily tell who had compatible personality traits. Our test was a combination of a Skillset and Personality Assessment and it has allowed us to confidently interview viable applicants. While most companies at the Career Fair were still relying on a standard resume and biased interview, Marcus Engineering proved to be one step ahead.

item.title: Product Engineering Requirements Guide
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Requirements Why? What? How?

There is often confusion among our customers regarding how to formulate a contract, a statement of work and an engagement for design services.  The confusion often lies in 3 simple question “Who? What? and Why?”

Making these three questions clear to your consulting firm can greatly streamline the engagement process, will reduce costs, and alleviate miscommunication and frustration.

One secret to lower pricing is being on top of these principles.  A company that comes across as experienced with product development, communication, and requirements planning will always convey to the customer  lower risk and smother development, meaning  a decrease in both cost and frustration.

item.title: IEC-62304, 13485, 60601, Oh My!
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
As a medical device specialist with Microchip, Marcus Engineer frequently incorporates Microchip products into medical instrumentation.   Often customers ask: “Are Microchip’s microprocessors [and other components] compliant with (insert standard here)?”

IEC-62304 “Medical Device Software – Software Lifecycle Process”,  ISO-13485 “Medical Devices – Quality Management Systems”, and IEC-60601-1 “Medical Electrical Equipment” are sources of great heartburn for customers with strong technical backgrounds, but infrequent exposure to regulated product development.

So, to answer your question: “Are Microchip parts compliant with 62304, 13485, and/or 60601”.  Well… the answer is… IT DEPENDS!

The key to understanding these regulatory requirements isn’t a matter of hunting for compliant hardware and software, but in fact, it’s a matter of truly understanding the “intended use” of the parts, and the risks involved.  The risk, and use of the components selected are the determining factors in identify ultimate compliance with regulatory standards.

Does the product you are developing present a direct or indirect risk to the patient or user?  Does the failure of a component (such as a microprocessor or it’s software) potentially cause the product to cause direct or indirect harm to the patient?  If the answer is yes, then you need to determine the likelihood of failure, and the impact of such a failure.  Only then can you determine if a design or selection of components is appropriate.

Determining the appropriateness of Microchip parts for a medical device design can be summarized in some time consuming, but otherwise straight forward steps:

  • PRODUCT RISK – Determine the risks associated with the product (ISO-14971 is a good reference)
  • ARCHITECTURE – Rough out the high level system architecture for the electronics and software
  • ARCHITECTURE RISK – Again, consider the risks and examine the risk associated with each block of your system architecture.  What happens if each block fails in the worst possible way.
  • MITIGATE – If risks are high (moderate to high likelihood and moderate to high impact) consider simplification, redundancy, self checks and other mechanisms to reduce the impact or likelihood of a risk.  This could be a change to how the device is used, separation of embedded functions, or addition of some redundancy.

The above checklist and documentation are what drive compliance with IEC-60601, ISO-13485, and IEC-62304.  The specific choice of components remains at the discretion of the designer, and with the guidance of the risk analysis and mitigations.

Microchip, in fact, is a very strong partner for medical device development.  Microchip encourages and supports medical device development while other major semiconductor manufacturers won’t even discuss your project with you. 

Marcus Engineering is also available to assist with all aspects of medical device development having a long track record of regulatory compliance success.  Medical Device development is detailed and process driven, and challenging, but it doesn’t have to be overwhelming.  Like any design, the proper understanding, background and context are critical to a smooth development process.

The contents of this article are not design recommendations, instructions, or formula for regulatory compliance or an indication of suitability of components or design approach.  The reader is responsible for appropriate consideration of design risks, regulatory standards, and regulatory compliance.  Marcus Engineering and Microchip do not accept any liability, and do not provide and warranty implied or otherwise associated with the content herein.

item.title: Requirements: Why They Matter
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
"Why do requirements matter?  I just want to build things!"
- Every Engineer... EVER!

What we often don’t realize is that we are always working from a set of requirements, no matter how simple the project.  Project requirements are a great way to suck the ad-hoc engineering FUN out of any project but they are a necessary evil and in fact save tons of time headache and embarrassment both in a hobby as well as a professional setting. 

If you are on track to transition from a hobby maker to a product developer for an entrepreneurial endeavor losing time, and money due to redesign because you didn’t meet requirements, or because requirements were vague can make the difference between a successful startup and one that just didn’t quite make it and ran out of money.

Let’s take a simple hobby example.  I’ve always gotten a kick of of neopixels.  For those of you who don’t know what a neopixel is check them out HERE.  Neopixels are low power RGB LEDs that are addressable such that you can have a long string of nearly as many as you like and control them from an FPGA, Microprocessor, Arduino, Raspberry Pi etc.

I would love to set up a strip of neopixels around the upper edges of my lab to give a fun ambiance and perhaps sync to techno music I might play.  So, as an avid hobbyist I’d go to Sparkfun and buy:

  • 5x 6’ neopixel light strips
  • 1A 5V wall wart
  • Arduino UNO
  • Microphone

So will my project work?  Turns out it won’t.  I blew it.  Power supply was ridiculously small, I didn’t have enough memory on the Arduino Uno, I didn’t buy enough LED strips, and never got to the microphone.

I could have avoided having to spend too much time and money rebuilding and rebuying parts if I had simply sketched out requirements for my project:

General Requirements

  1. Lots of color fidelity
    1. 24 bit
  2. Fast enough to be smooth with music
    1. Generally we should be at a few 10s of milliseconds latency to track music well (trust me on this one)
  3. Enough light to light the room pretty well
    1. Need to look into this but assuming 1 neopixel per inch is enough

Neopixels

  1. 1 neopixel per inch (I’m really just guessing here but some data to support this would be wise)
  2. 12’x16’’ office
    1. Perimeter will require 56’ of neopixel strips
    1. => 672 neopixels
  3. 800KHz max bit rate
    1. ~30 microseconds per pixel update plus 50 us after each string of updates
      1. 20.2ms per update

Processor

  1. 24 bit color
  2. 3 bytes of ram per pixel for storage => 2016 bytes of RAM

Power

  1. Each neopixel uses 60mA max 20mA nominal
    1. 40A max 13A nominal

So I never really built this project for my office.  I’ve been engineering for a long time but I shot gunned my assumptions when I made the list to order and CLEARLY I blew it.  Not having enough RAM and the current requirements really surprised me when I actually went through requirements and did some prelim calculations before buying parts and digging into design.

Granted this was just an exercise but it illustrates what can go wrong even on a simple project.  Imagine a larger project and you can see how the errors and assumptions would multiply.  This becomes even more significant with large projects or if you are working with multiple independent teams on one project.

Next time we’ll talk in more detail about types of requirements and how to differentiate between requirements and specifications.

item.title: The Entrepreneurial Minds of Tomorrow Are FORGE(D) In Arizona
item.dc:creator: ehopper
item.content:encoded:
Turning solar farms into gardens, and boat engines into renewable generators are just a few of the business plans coalescing in downtown Tucson AZ at the Arizona FORGE. Ideas like these and their underlying business plans for growth are tested and evolved at the "FORGE," a program at the University of Arizona.  

FORGE opened downtown in late 2019 with the intent of forging and tempering companies in their incubator and accelerator programs. The acronym forge stands for "Finding Opportunities and Resources to Grow Entrepreneurs".

One of the resources for the growth of entrepreneurs is a strong mentor and resource network.  As one of the mentors for Forge Dr. Marcus, President of Marcus Engineering, advises many of the startup companies enrolled in FORGE.

On December 5th 2020 Dr. Marcus participated in a pitch day and mock board meetings with several companies including Black Oak Investments ”A green energy investment company specializing in solar farm development, habitat restoration, and community energy resilience." And Hydro-Gen Systems whose founder, James Magras, noted that "(they) build better boat propulsion systems, giving boat owners the worry-free experience, they are looking for out on the water by combining the reliability and power of electric motors with the unparalleled energy reserve of a hydrogen fuel cells."

The mock board meeting event at FORGE was broken up into two parts: final team pitches and mock board meetings. The final team pitches gave an overview and insight into the plans and challenges the startups face helping to orient participating mentors, while the mock board meetings allowed the teams to obtain guidance into the most significant strategic challenges they face as a company and gain experience leading a board meeting.

What excites Dr. Marcus about FORGE is that “FORGE is a community resource that accelerates our community's ability to evaluate, advice, and slingshot local startups" FORGE'S success has been driven by Brian Ellerman, the founding Director at FORGE and an experienced successful entrepreneur himself.

One of the most valuable tools is learning from others' previous mistakes, and helping entrepreneurs avoid those mistakes in the future.  It is easy for Entrepreneurial Minds to have their head in the clouds and not adjust to real-world economic environments, and thus it is essential to have mentors to help them keep at least one foot on the ground.   Dr. Marcus is one of those key mentors known for bringing real-world approaches and realistic goals to startups, especially when it comes to engineering and product development.  "There are fine lines between what can be envisioned, what can be built, and what can be practical in the marketplace.  If you are on the wrong side of those lines a lot of business planning can become moot."
